revision.text:
'''越塚御門古墳'''（こしつかごもんこふん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]大字越に所在する古墳である。[[2010年]][[12月9日]]にその発見が発表された。[[牽牛子塚古墳]]に隣接する[[八角墳]]（八角形墳）であり、[[日本書紀]]の記述から[[大田皇女]]の墓である可能性が高いと言われている。
== 発見の経緯 == 2010年10月中旬、牽牛子塚古墳周辺の発掘現場を埋め戻す最中、明日香村教育委員会の委員である[[西光慎治]]は、古墳の南東約20メートルに埋まっていた1メートルほどの石に人為的に割れた痕跡があったため、気になってその周辺を発掘したことが発見に繋がった([http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ 産経新聞2010年12月9日])。
== 宮内庁の反応 == [[宮内庁]]は越塚御門古墳の発見後も、[[車木ケンノウ古墳]]付近にある墳土を大田皇女の陵墓とする指定を変更する姿勢を見せていない。理由として、日本書紀の「[[天智天皇|中大兄皇子]]が石槨を造る労役を民に課さなかった」という記述と一致しないためとしている（[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html 毎日新聞2010年12月10日]）。
{{Japanese-history-stub|こしつかこもんこふん}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こしつかこもんこふん}} [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:八角墳]] [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:明日香村]]

revision.comment: 本文中の外部リンクを脚注形式に変更しました。
revision.text:
'''越塚御門古墳'''（こしつかごもんこふん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]大字越に所在する古墳である。[[2010年]][[12月9日]]にその発見が発表された。[[牽牛子塚古墳]]に隣接する[[八角墳]]（八角形墳）であり、[[日本書紀]]の記述から[[大田皇女]]の墓である可能性が高いと言われている。
== 発見の経緯 == 2010年10月中旬、牽牛子塚古墳周辺の発掘現場を埋め戻す最中、明日香村教育委員会の技師である[[西光慎治]]は、古墳の南東約20メートルに埋まっていた1メートルほどの石に人為的に割れた痕跡があったため、気になってその周辺を発掘したことが発見に繋がった<ref>[http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ 牽牛子塚古墳 「こんな足もとに」思わぬ大発見 」：イザ！ 産経新聞2010年12月9日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 宮内庁の反応 == [[宮内庁]]は越塚御門古墳の発見後も、[[車木ケンノウ古墳]]付近にある墳土を大田皇女の陵墓とする指定を変更する姿勢を見せていない。理由として、日本書紀の「[[天智天皇|中大兄皇子]]が石槨を造る労役を民に課さなかった」という記述と一致しないためとしている<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html 越塚御門古墳：日本書紀通りに発見 陵墓見直し論、加速も 毎日新聞2010年12月10日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 脚注 == <references/>
{{Japanese-history-stub|こしつかこもんこふん}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こしつかこもんこふん}} [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:八角墳]] [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:明日香村]]

'''越塚御門古墳'''（こしつかごもんこふん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]大字越に所在する[[古墳]]である。[[2010年]][[12月9日]]にその発見が発表された。[[牽牛子塚古墳]]に隣接する[[八角墳]]（八角形墳）であり、『[[日本書紀]]』の記述から[[飛鳥時代]]の[[皇族]]である[[大田皇女]]の墓である可能性が高いと言われている。
== 発見の経緯 == 2010年10月中旬、牽牛子塚古墳周辺の発掘現場を埋め戻す最中、明日香村教育委員会の技師である[[西光慎治]]は、古墳の南東約20メートルに埋まっていた1メートルほどの石に人為的に割れた痕跡があったため、気になってその周辺を発掘したことが発見に繋がった<ref>[http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ 牽牛子塚古墳 「こんな足もとに」思わぬ大発見 」：イザ！ 産経新聞2010年12月9日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 宮内庁の反応 == [[宮内庁]]は越塚御門古墳の発見後も、[[車木ケンノウ古墳]]（宮内庁により[[皇極天皇|斉明天皇]]の陵とされている）付近にある墳土を大田皇女の陵墓とする指定を変更する姿勢を見せていない。理由として、『日本書紀』の「[[天智天皇|中大兄皇子]]が石槨を造る労役を民に課さなかった」という記述と一致しないためとしている<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html 越塚御門古墳：日本書紀通りに発見 陵墓見直し論、加速も 毎日新聞2010年12月10日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 脚注 == <references/>
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こしつかこもんこふん}} [[Category:古墳時代]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:八角墳]] [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:明日香村]]

== 宮内庁の反応 == [[宮内庁]]は越塚御門古墳の発見後も、[[車木ケンノウ古墳]]（宮内庁により[[皇極天皇|斉明天皇]]の陵とされている）付近にある墳土を大田皇女の陵墓とする指定を変更する姿勢を見せていない。理由として、『日本書紀』の「[[天智天皇|中大兄皇子]]が石槨を造る労役を民に課さなかった」という記述と一致しないためとしている<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html 越塚御門古墳：日本書紀通りに発見 陵墓見直し論、加速も 毎日新聞2010年12月10日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。

{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こしつかこもんこふん}} [[Category:八角墳]] [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:明日香村]]

revision.comment: 国指定文化財等データベース
国の史跡に係る事柄の加筆、画像除去（推定復元過程に著作権の発生する模型であるが複製・再配布許可が不明瞭のため）、八角墳の文言除去（牽牛子塚古墳との混同と判断）。
revision.text:
'''越塚御門古墳'''（こしつかごもんこふん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]大字越にある[[古墳]]。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
[[2010年]][[12月9日]]にその発見が発表された。[[牽牛子塚古墳]]に隣接する古墳であり、『[[日本書紀]]』の記述から[[飛鳥時代]]の[[皇族]]である[[大田皇女]]の墓である可能性が高いと言われている。
== 発見の経緯 == 2010年10月中旬、牽牛子塚古墳周辺の発掘現場を埋め戻す最中、明日香村教育委員会の技師である[[西光慎治]]は、古墳の南東約20メートルに埋まっていた1メートルほどの石に人為的に割れた痕跡があったため、気になってその周辺を発掘したことが発見に繋がった<ref>[http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ 牽牛子塚古墳 「こんな足もとに」思わぬ大発見 」：イザ！ 産経新聞2010年12月9日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 宮内庁の反応 == [[宮内庁]]は越塚御門古墳の発見後も、[[車木ケンノウ古墳]]（宮内庁により[[皇極天皇|斉明天皇]]の陵とされている）付近にある墳土を大田皇女の陵墓とする指定を変更する姿勢を見せていない。理由として、『日本書紀』の「[[天智天皇|中大兄皇子]]が石槨を造る労役を民に課さなかった」という記述と一致しないためとしている<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html 越塚御門古墳：日本書紀通りに発見 陵墓見直し論、加速も 毎日新聞2010年12月10日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 文化財 == === 国の史跡 === * 牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳 - 2014年（平成26年）3月18日に既指定の史跡「牽牛子塚古墳」に越塚御門古墳を追加して、指定名称を「牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳」に変更<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}

== 発見の経緯 == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|石槨俯瞰図}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|石槨展開図}}]] 2010年10月中旬、牽牛子塚古墳周辺の発掘現場を埋め戻す最中、明日香村教育委員会の技師である[[西光慎治]]は、古墳の南東約20メートルに埋まっていた1メートルほどの石に人為的に割れた痕跡があったため、気になってその周辺を発掘したことが発見に繋がった<ref>[http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ 牽牛子塚古墳 「こんな足もとに」思わぬ大発見 」：イザ！ 産経新聞2010年12月9日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 宮内庁の反応 == [[宮内庁]]は越塚御門古墳の発見後も、[[車木ケンノウ古墳]]（宮内庁により[[斉明天皇]]の陵とされている）付近にある墳土を大田皇女の陵墓とする指定を変更する姿勢を見せていない。理由として、『日本書紀』の「[[天智天皇|中大兄皇子]]が石槨を造る労役を民に課さなかった」という記述と一致しないためとしている<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html 越塚御門古墳：日本書紀通りに発見 陵墓見直し論、加速も 毎日新聞2010年12月10日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 文化財 == === 国の史跡 === * 牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳 - 2014年（平成26年）3月18日に既指定の史跡「牽牛子塚古墳」に越塚御門古墳を追加して、指定名称を「牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳」に変更<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こしつかこもんこふん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:奈良県にある国指定の史跡]]

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|越塚御門古墳 石槨}}]] '''越塚御門古墳'''（こしつかごもんこふん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]大字越にある[[古墳]]。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
[[2010年]][[12月9日]]にその発見が発表された。[[牽牛子塚古墳]]に隣接する古墳であり、『[[日本書紀]]』の記述から[[飛鳥時代]]の[[皇族]]である[[大田皇女]]の墓である可能性が高いと言われている。
== 発見の経緯 == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|石槨俯瞰図}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|石槨展開図}}]] 2010年10月中旬、牽牛子塚古墳周辺の発掘現場を埋め戻す最中、明日香村教育委員会の技師である[[西光慎治]]は、古墳の南東約20メートルに埋まっていた1メートルほどの石に人為的に割れた痕跡があったため、気になってその周辺を発掘したことが発見に繋がった<ref>[http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ 牽牛子塚古墳 「こんな足もとに」思わぬ大発見 」：イザ！ 産経新聞2010年12月9日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 宮内庁の反応 == [[宮内庁]]は越塚御門古墳の発見後も、[[車木ケンノウ古墳]]（宮内庁により[[斉明天皇]]の陵とされている）付近にある墳土を大田皇女の陵墓とする指定を変更する姿勢を見せていない。理由として、『日本書紀』の「[[天智天皇|中大兄皇子]]が石槨を造る労役を民に課さなかった」という記述と一致しないためとしている<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html 越塚御門古墳：日本書紀通りに発見 陵墓見直し論、加速も 毎日新聞2010年12月10日] 2010年12月閲覧</ref>。
== 文化財 == === 国の史跡 === * 牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳 - 2014年（平成26年）3月18日に既指定の史跡「牽牛子塚古墳」に越塚御門古墳を追加して、指定名称を「牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳」に変更<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>。

revision.text:
[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|en|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Ōta no Himemiko|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * **Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun** - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Asuka, Nara]] [[Category:National Historic Sites in Nara Prefecture]] [[ja:越塚御門古墳]]

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|en|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Ōta no Himemiko|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * **Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun** - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|en|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Ōta no Himemiko|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Ōta no Himemiko|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}

revision.text:
[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.

Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}

[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried approximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried aqqroximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

Accessed December 2010.</ref>

The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}

revision.text:
[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried aqqroximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, sekkaku.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun Stone Chamber}}]] '''Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' (越塚御門古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, Japan. It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.
== Discovery == [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Expanded view of the stone chamber}}]] In mid-October 2010, during the process of backfilling the excavation site around the Kengoshizuka Kofun, {{ill|Shinji Saikō|ja|西光慎治}}, a technician with the Asuka Village Board of Education, noticed a one-meter stone buried aqqroximately 20 meters southeast of the Kengoshizuka Kofun. The stone showed clear evidence of human modification, prompting further excavation that led to the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20101214104644/http://www.iza.ne.jp/news/newsarticle/natnews/topics/472754/ "Unexpected Major Discovery Near Kengoshizuka Kofun"], Sankei Shimbun, December 9, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Response from the Imperial Household Agency == Despite the discovery of the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has not revised its designation of the tumulus near the {{ill|Kuramoki no Misasagi|ja|車木ケンノウ古墳}} (currently identified by the agency as the mausoleum of {{ill|Empress Saimei|ja|斉明天皇}}) as the burial site of Ōta no Himemiko. The agency cites discrepancies between the archaeological findings and the Nihon Shoki's account, which states that {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} did not impose forced labor on the populace to construct the stone chamber.<ref>[http://mainichi.jp/enta/art/news/20101210ddm012040038000c.html "Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun: Discovered as Described in the Nihon Shoki; Debate Over Imperial Mausoleum Designations Accelerates"], Mainichi Shimbun, December 10, 2010. Accessed December 2010.</ref>
== Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun''' - In 2014 (Heisei 26), the previously designated Historic Site "Kengoshizuka Kofun" was expanded to include the Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun, and the designation was renamed "Kengoshizuka Kofun and Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun."<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Koshi Tsukagomon Kofun}} * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|1945|牽牛子塚古墳・越塚御門古墳}}

It has been designated a {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan}}}.
The discovery of the tumulus was announced on {{ill|December 9, 2010|JA|12月9日}}, and it is situated adjacent to the {{ill|Kengoshizuka Kofun|ja|牽牛子塚古墳}}. Based on descriptions in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, it is believed to be the probable burial site of {{ill|Princess Ōta|ja|大田皇女}}, a member of the Imperial family from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}.

Accessed December 2010.</ref>

Accessed December 2010.</ref>
